metadata.summary: LoRA fine-tuning with PEFT+TRL: freeze base weights, inject low-rank adapters (rank 8-64), reduce parameters 100-250x. Includes QLoRA, multi-adapter management, production LoRATrainingPipeline.

content.title: Fine-tuning (LoRA)
content.summary: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) fine-tuning methodology using PEFT + TRL: freezing base model weights and injecting small trainable matrices (rank 8-64) into attention layers, reducing trainable parameters by 100-250x. Covers QLoRA (4-bit quantization), multiple-adapter management, hyperparameter search, and the LoRATrainingPipeline production class.

why.p: LoRA enables fine-tuning on consumer GPUs (8-24GB VRAM) where full fine-tuning requires 80GB+. The adapter approach preserves general capabilities (frozen base) while specializing behavior for the target task. Multiple adapters can share one base model and be switched at runtime — critical for multi-tenant or multi-task production deployments.

list.item: Consumer GPU available (8-24GB VRAM) and full fine-tuning is not feasible
Need to maintain multiple task-specific adapters on one base model without reloading weights
Quick iteration experiments where training must complete in hours, not days
Adapting model style/behavior without degrading general capabilities
Production scenario where a specialized smaller model is cheaper to serve than a large prompted model
Task requires injecting new factual knowledge — LoRA adapters do not reliably encode facts; use RAG
Dataset is smaller than 50 examples — adapter overfits immediately; use few-shot prompting
Target inference server does not support on-the-fly adapter loading — merge the adapter first
Real-time latency requirement below 100ms — adapter overhead adds ~5-10ms; merging eliminates this
Multiple simultaneous LoRA experiments on the same GPU — they will OOM; serialize jobs

rule.statement: Print trainable parameter count immediately after get_peft_model() — if it shows 0, stop and fix target_modules before starting training.
rule.rationale: Wrong target_modules names cause 0 trainable parameters; training proceeds silently for hours producing a model identical to the base model.

example.title: Correct LoRA Config and Parameter Check
example.code:
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
lora_config = LoraConfig( r=16, lora_alpha=16, target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"], lora_dropout=0.05, bias="none", task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM, ) model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
def check_lora_params(model) -> dict: trainable = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad) total = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()) if trainable == 0: raise ValueError("No trainable params — check target_modules names") return {"trainable": trainable, "total": total, "pct": f"{100 * trainable / total:.4f}%"}
stats = check_lora_params(model) print(stats) # Expected: trainable% between 0.01% and 2%
example.note: Raises immediately if target_modules are wrong, preventing hours of wasted GPU time.

rule.statement: Set lora_alpha equal to lora_r as a default; only deviate after observing training dynamics.
rule.rationale: The scaling factor lora_alpha/r controls effective learning rate for the adapter; equal values provide a neutral baseline that works for most tasks.

list.item: rank 8: minimal memory, simple extraction or classification tasks
rank 16: general-purpose default for instruction following and style adaptation
rank 32: complex tasks requiring nuanced behavioral changes
rank 64+: rarely justified; use only when rank 32 validation loss is still plateauing with >5k examples

rule.statement: Always set tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token before training if the tokenizer has no pad token.
rule.rationale: Missing pad token causes silent data corruption in batched training; the model trains on misaligned sequences.

rule.statement: Save the tokenizer alongside the adapter: tokenizer.save_pretrained(output_dir) — never save the adapter alone.
rule.rationale: An adapter saved without the tokenizer cannot be reproduced or shared; model IDs without matching tokenizers are not deployable.

rule.statement: Call prepare_model_for_kbit_training() BEFORE get_peft_model() — reversing the order causes silent training failure.
rule.rationale: prepare_model_for_kbit_training() configures gradient checkpointing and layer normalization for 4-bit training; applying LoRA before this produces incompatible layer states.

example.title: QLoRA Configuration with Correct Ordering
example.code:
from transformers import BitsAndBytesConfig, AutoModelForCausalLM from peft import prepare_model_for_kbit_training, get_peft_model import torch
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig( load_in_4bit=True, bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4", bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16, bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True, ) model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained( "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B", quantization_config=bnb_config, device_map="auto", ) model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model) # MUST come before get_peft_model model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
example.note: bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=bfloat16 avoids NaN loss on A100/H100. Use float16 on older GPUs.

list.item: 7B model: full FT 28GB → LoRA fp16 16GB → QLoRA 4-bit 6-8GB
13B model: full FT 52GB → LoRA fp16 28GB → QLoRA 4-bit 10-12GB
70B model: full FT 280GB → LoRA fp16 140GB → QLoRA 4-bit 40-48GB

rule.statement: Use paged_adamw_8bit optimizer only when bitsandbytes is installed and CUDA is available — fall back to adamw_torch for CPU training.
rule.rationale: paged_adamw_8bit is a bitsandbytes-specific optimizer that uses paged memory to handle optimizer state spikes; it is not available in non-CUDA environments.

rule.statement: Use SFTTrainer from TRL instead of raw HF Trainer — it handles dataset formatting, sample packing, and LoRA integration more reliably.
rule.rationale: Raw Trainer requires manual dataset tokenization, label masking, and data collator setup. SFTTrainer automates these with proven defaults and supports peft_config directly.

rule.statement: Set evaluation_strategy="epoch" only when an eval_dataset is also provided — setting the strategy without the dataset raises a ValueError at epoch end, wasting compute.
rule.rationale: The error is not raised at job start but at epoch end; a full epoch of training is wasted before the crash.

rule.statement: Merge LoRA adapter into base model weights with model.merge_and_unload() before deploying to vLLM or TGI — adapter overhead is zero after merge.
rule.rationale: vLLM requires a standard HF model; PEFT-wrapped models cause startup errors. Merge is lossless and removes the adapter lookup overhead from every forward pass.

example.title: Merge LoRA Adapter for Deployment
example.code:
from peft import PeftModel from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM import torch
# Load base + adapter, merge, save as standard HF model base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained( "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B", torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map="auto", ) model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "./lora-adapter") merged = model.merge_and_unload() merged.save_pretrained("./merged_model") # Deploy merged_model to vLLM as a standard model
example.note: merge_and_unload() is lossless — merged weights are mathematically identical to adapter-applied weights.

rule.statement: Keep adapters separate for multi-task workflows; do not merge adapters trained on incompatible tasks into the same base model.
rule.rationale: Merging two adapters trained on different tasks can produce weight interference; task-specific adapters should be switched at runtime, not merged.

rule.statement: For multi-adapter runtime switching, load adapters with model.load_adapter(path, adapter_name=name) and activate with model.set_adapter(name).
rule.rationale: PeftModel supports multiple adapters sharing one base model instance; switching adapters at runtime avoids loading the base model multiple times.

section.name: Agentic Workflow and Integration
section.p: Claude subagents handle the non-GPU portions of the LoRA pipeline: generating training data, selecting rank and target modules based on model family and task type, constructing the LoraConfig, and writing the training script. The actual trainer.train() call is dispatched to a GPU instance via shell command or cloud API. Post-training, an agent can load the adapter, run sample inferences, and decide whether to merge-and-export or discard. Human review is required before merging adapters into a production base model.

list.item: faion-sdd-execution — generate LoRA config from task description and model family, validate target_modules match model architecture
Custom eval agent — load adapter, run 20-30 representative test prompts, score outputs, return pass/fail

section.name: Prompt Pattern
section.p: Given model family "{model_family}" and task type "{task_type}", select:

list.item: Appropriate lora_rank (8/16/32/64)
target_modules list (provide exact names for this architecture)
learning_rate
Whether to include MLP layers

section.p: Output JSON: {rank, alpha, target_modules, learning_rate, rationale}

Examine these 5 sample outputs from the LoRA-tuned model vs the base model. Score each on: format_adherence (0-1), content_quality (0-1), regression_risk (0-1). Flag any output where regression_risk > 0.7 for human review.

list.item: Print trainable parameter count immediately after get_peft_model() — if it shows 0, stop and fix target_modules
Use SFTTrainer from TRL instead of raw Trainer — it handles dataset formatting, packing, and LoRA integration more reliably
Set use_gradient_checkpointing="unsloth" when using Unsloth — saves ~30% VRAM vs. standard gradient checkpointing
Always save tokenizer alongside adapter: model.save_pretrained(path) + tokenizer.save_pretrained(path)
When doing hyperparameter search, vary rank (8/16/32) first; alpha usually set equal to rank is fine for initial experiments
Merge the adapter before deployment to vLLM: model.merge_and_unload() — this has zero quality loss and removes adapter overhead
For multi-adapter workflows: keep adapters separate, load at runtime; do not mix-merge adapters trained on incompatible tasks
prepare_model_for_kbit_training() must be called before get_peft_model() for QLoRA; reversing order causes silent training failure
GPU OOM during training is recoverable by reducing per_device_train_batch_size to 1 and increasing gradient_accumulation_steps proportionally — an agent can attempt this automatically
Adapter checkpointing writes partial files during training; an agent reading an intermediate checkpoint will get a corrupt model — always load from the final output_dir
Multiple simultaneous LoRA experiments on the same GPU will OOM — agents must serialize GPU jobs, not parallelize them
evaluation_strategy="epoch" with no eval_dataset raises a ValueError at epoch end, not at job start — a common source of wasted compute
peft — LoRA/QLoRA adapter creation and management | pip install peft · huggingface.co/docs/peft
trl — SFTTrainer, DPOTrainer, ORPOTrainer | pip install trl · huggingface.co/docs/trl
bitsandbytes — 4-bit/8-bit quantization for QLoRA | pip install bitsandbytes · github.com/TimDettmers/bitsandbytes
accelerate — Multi-GPU training launcher | pip install accelerate · huggingface.co/docs/accelerate
mergekit — Merge LoRA adapter into base model weights | pip install mergekit · github.com/arcee-ai/mergekit
unsloth — 2x faster LoRA, direct GGUF export | pip install unsloth · github.com/unslothai/unsloth
Hugging Face Hub (SaaS/OSS, agent-friendly via SDK) — Push/pull adapters; model.push_to_hub()
Weights & Biases (SaaS, agent-friendly via REST) — Loss tracking, adapter artifact registry
RunPod (SaaS, partial agent support) — GPU pod launch via API; SSH for training
Modal (SaaS, agent-friendly via Python SDK) — Serverless GPU; run trainer.train() as a Modal function
Ollama (OSS, agent-friendly via REST :11434) — Serve merged GGUF; agent calls /api/generate
vLLM (OSS, agent-friendly via OpenAI API) — Serve merged model; requires merge before deployment

template.purpose: LoRATrainingPipeline class with QLoRA, SFTTrainer, and merge-and-unload
Verify trainable parameter count after applying LoRA; raises if 0
Prompt for selecting rank, target_modules, and learning rate given model family and task
